main_summary.summary: Oliver W. Hill Jr., son of NAACP civil rights attorney Oliver W. Hill Sr., was interviewed in Richmond, Virginia on August 17, 2013 by David Cline for the Civil Rights History Project. Born in 1949, Hill Jr. grew up in segregated Richmond, where his father practiced with Spottswood W. Robinson III on cases tied to the NAACP Legal Defense and Educational Fund (LDF). He recounts the household environment of telephone threats and a cross burning, and traces his father's path from Roanoke to Dunbar High School in Washington and Howard Law School under Charles Hamilton Houston and Mordecai Wyatt Johnson, where his father was a classmate of Thurgood Marshall. Hill Jr. describes the Farmville student walkout led by Barbara Rose Johns and the resulting Davis v. County School Board of Prince Edward County case, the strategic pivot from separate-but-equal to attacking segregation per se, and the Byrd Machine's massive resistance under Governor J. Lindsay Almond Jr. He was one of the first Black students in Richmond to desegregate the schools, entering Chandler Middle School in junior high. He attended Howard University in the late 1960s, studied Eastern philosophy and meditation, became a meditation teacher in Boston and Detroit, then earned a psychology graduate degree at Michigan and built his career at Virginia State University, later collaborating with Bob Moses and the Algebra Project in Petersburg.
key_themes: NAACP Legal Defense Fund litigation strategy
Howard Law School and Charles Hamilton Houston's training
Barbara Rose Johns and the Farmville student walkout
Massive resistance and Richmond school desegregation
Black education and the Algebra Project
main_summary.historical_significance:
Hill Jr. offers first-person testimony on the domestic and legal infrastructure behind the Brown v. Board cases from inside the Hill household: the rotating residence of NAACP lawyers who could not stay in segregated Richmond hotels, the breakfast-table strategy sessions involving Thurgood Marshall and Robert L. Carter, and his father's incremental campaign to overturn Plessy v. Ferguson. He provides direct recollection of Barbara Rose Johns's call to his father, the strategic shift from separate-but-equal to attacking segregation per se, and the family connection between Barbara Rose Johns and her uncle Vernon Johns.

The interview also documents one Black child's experience of being placed in the desegregating Richmond schools roughly seven years after Brown, the social geometry of Chandler Middle School in the early 1960s, the later Judge Robert R. Merhige Jr. busing decision that included surrounding counties, and the William Rehnquist non-recusal that produced the 4-4 Supreme Court split overturning the cross-county remedy. Hill Jr.'s post-movement trajectory through Howard during the black power era, meditation teaching, psychology research at Virginia State University, and partnership with Bob Moses on the Algebra Project links the civil rights legal generation to contemporary education reform in Petersburg.

chapters.part: A Richmond Childhood Behind the Threats
chapters.title: It's Your Interview, You're in Charge
chapters.summary: David Cline of Virginia Tech and the Southern Oral History Program opens the Civil Rights History Project session in Richmond, Virginia, on August 17, 2013, with John Bishop behind the camera, explaining his chronological family-history approach before introducing Oliver W. Hill Jr.

chapters.part: A Richmond Childhood Behind the Threats
chapters.title: A Circumscribed World
chapters.summary: Hill recounts being born in 1949 in Richmond's Black hospital, now a Virginia Union University dorm, and the self-contained, class-heterogeneous Black neighborhood where a lawyer lived beside a plumber and one rarely needed to leave, the hub of his first dozen years.

chapters.part: A Richmond Childhood Behind the Threats
chapters.title: Somebody Else's Pathology
chapters.summary: Hill describes his father's NAACP Legal Defense Fund work, the telephone threats that kept him from answering the phone and a cross burning when he was six or seven, his parents' effort to teach him that prejudice was others' pathology, and the white-normed media that still shaped a boy who wanted wavy hair like Elvis Presley.

chapters.part: His Father's Road to the Law
chapters.title: Raised by the Pentecosts
chapters.summary: Hill traces his father Oliver Hill Sr., born in Richmond in 1907, deserted by his own father, raised partly by a stern, well-read foster family named Pentecost in Roanoke while his mother took seasonal hotel work, and sent to Washington's prestigious Dunbar High School because Roanoke had no Black high school.

chapters.part: His Father's Road to the Law
chapters.title: Social Engineer or Social Parasite
chapters.topic: Howard Law and the Plessy Strategy
chapters.summary: Hill tells how an uncle's annotated constitution made his father question segregation's legality and drew him to Howard Law, remade by President Mordecai Wyatt Johnson and dean Charles Hamilton Houston, where classmate Thurgood Marshall and Houston's charge to be social engineers launched the strategy of attacking Plessy v. Ferguson.

chapters.part: His Father's Road to the Law
chapters.title: Waiting Tables in the Depression
chapters.topic: Building the LDF and the War
chapters.summary: Hill recounts his father graduating in 1933, waiting tables in Depression-era Roanoke, joining the close Howard-graduate circle of NAACP Legal Defense and Educational Fund lawyers, winning the Virginia teacher salary case, and being drafted in his forties for World War II while repeatedly demanding officer training.

chapters.part: His Father's Road to the Law
chapters.title: Knocking on White Doors
chapters.summary: Hill describes his father serving in an all-Black unit in England and France, then returning to become the first Black person elected to office in the South since Reconstruction on the Richmond City Council, canvassing white neighborhoods, losing a House of Delegates race by about forty votes, and attending every political meeting in town.

chapters.part: Brown and the Virginia Cases
chapters.title: Tar Paper Shacks in Farmville
chapters.summary: Hill recounts the call from Barbara Rose Johns, a high school girl who organized a walkout over Farmville's tar-paper-shack conditions, and how her persistence drew his father to a parents' meeting where he warned the case would now attack segregation per se, making Davis v. County School Board of Prince Edward County part of the Brown package.

chapters.part: Brown and the Virginia Cases
chapters.title: Ten Years Before Rosa Parks
chapters.summary: Hill notes that the Davis case took from 1952 to the 1954 Brown decision, that Barbara Rose Johns suffered and left Farmville, that her uncle was the preacher Vernon Johns who preceded Martin Luther King in Alabama, and that a decade before Rosa Parks his father's firm won the Irene Morgan case ending interstate transportation segregation.

chapters.part: Brown and the Virginia Cases
chapters.title: Strategy Sessions at the Breakfast Table
chapters.summary: Hill describes the lawyers' incremental two-decade strategy, traveling attorneys like Thurgood Marshall and Robert L. Carter lodging at the Hill home because hotels were closed to them, his father's partnership with Spottswood W. Robinson III, and Virginia's massive resistance under the Byrd Machine and Governor J. Lindsay Almond Jr.

chapters.part: One of the First to Desegregate
chapters.title: Desegregation but Not Integration
chapters.summary: Hill explains the post-Brown decade of compliance cases, his own place among the first Virginia students to desegregate schools around 1961, the pupil placement board that assigned schools, his father's good relationship with progressive school-board head Lewis Powell, and his father's distinction that a token few Black students made a school desegregated but not integrated.

chapters.part: One of the First to Desegregate
chapters.title: Walking Up the Empty Steps
chapters.summary: Hill recalls not wanting to leave his Black-school friends, officials measuring distances with yardsticks to exclude a friend three feet too far from Chandler Middle School, the surreal first day when his father's partner Henry L. Marsh III drove him to a deserted-looking white school where Black students were marched two by two to homerooms, and the football team that, over the years, turned even prejudiced classmates into teammates as a Black prom queen and easing social segregation followed.

chapters.part: White Flight and the Color of Class
chapters.title: A Four-to-Four Split
chapters.summary: Hill recounts busing as a remedy and Judge Robert R. Merhige Jr ordering cross-county busing despite threats and his dog being killed, then the Supreme Court's four-to-four split, after Lewis F. Powell Jr recused himself over his Richmond school ties while William Rehnquist did not, upholding the lower court and killing the county-inclusive busing plan.

chapters.part: White Flight and the Color of Class
chapters.title: Race Morphed Into Class
chapters.summary: Hill compares Louisville merging city and county and describes Richmond's white power structure annexing county land until the Voting Rights Act blocked it as vote dilution, then an annexation moratorium leaving the city land-locked, and the old racial politics of Virginia's independent cities now clothed as class conflict.

chapters.part: Howard and the Inward Turn
chapters.title: Tarzan Movies to African Civilizations
chapters.summary: A little miffed but persuaded of the cause, Hill describes walking to Chandler Middle School from Northside, then Howard University in the late sixties, an eye-opening African history class after a childhood that knew only Tarzan movies, and campus black power figures Stokely Carmichael and H. Rap Brown challenging King's nonviolence with black nationalism.

chapters.part: Howard and the Inward Turn
chapters.title: An Inward Turn to Indian Philosophy
chapters.summary: Hill recounts black nationalism holding sway a year or two before an inward turn to Eastern philosophy, a senior perception-psychology course on how belief constructs reality, becoming a meditation teacher in Boston and Detroit en route to the University of Michigan, and framing nonviolence and dignified non-reaction as a practice of mindfulness rooted in Gandhi and ahimsa.

chapters.part: What Integration Cost the Classroom
chapters.title: The Best and Brightest Left Teaching
chapters.summary: Hill argues integration cost Black schools their gifted women teachers as the women's movement opened other careers, displaced Black principals, and ended the mission-driven, class-mixed schools of the segregation era, leaving inner-city hubs of poverty, and points to Petersburg's lowest-performing schools, eroding tax base, and revolving superintendents as the result.

chapters.part: Monuments and a Father's Recognition
chapters.title: Slavery as a Benign Institution
chapters.summary: Hill recalls Virginia history class teaching slavery as a benign, family-like institution he knew was false, and a succession of governors beginning with L. Douglas Wilder adding the Black story to the record, acknowledging that more civil rights cases arose in Virginia than any other Southern state in a climate that, unlike Mississippi, let people feel safer standing up.

chapters.part: Monuments and a Father's Recognition
chapters.title: Arthur Ashe on Monument Avenue
chapters.topic: Confederate Statues and a New Memorial
chapters.summary: Hill describes Monument Avenue's towering Confederate generals facing north or south by their war fate, the uproar over adding an Arthur Ashe statue, an earlier objection to a Lincoln statue at the Tredegar Iron Works, and the Capitol-grounds Virginia civil rights memorial picturing Barbara Rose Johns, his father, and Robinson near a statue of Harry F. Byrd Sr.

chapters.part: Monuments and a Father's Recognition
chapters.title: His Name on the Old Finance Building
chapters.summary: Hill notes the irony of his father's name on the Capitol's old finance building, where Byrd's circle plotted massive resistance and where his father once had to fight segregated seating to take the bar exam, and recounts the national recognition his father received in his last decade, including the 1999 Presidential Medal of Freedom, before the interview turns to Hill's own career.

chapters.part: A Psychologist's Fight for the Mind
chapters.title: Orange and Blue Hand-Me-Downs
chapters.summary: Hill describes his career at Virginia State University, an HBCU whose orange-and-blue colors came from UVA football hand-me-downs, the politics of state funding, and his research on Black education and cognitive factors, including how early childhood stimulation and the once-fixed-now-malleable view of cognitive ability shape achievement.

chapters.part: A Psychologist's Fight for the Mind
chapters.title: A Civil Rights Approach to Math
chapters.topic: Bob Moses and the Algebra Project
chapters.summary: Hill recounts partnering with Bob Moses and the Algebra Project in Petersburg, Moses being a sixties Mississippi voter-registration organizer who earned a Harvard math PhD and taught abstract math through children's life experiences while applying civil rights community-organizing to school reform, and an interview with Los Angeles educator Mildred Pitts Walter who wrote her own Black children's books.

chapters.part: A Psychologist's Fight for the Mind
chapters.title: A Framework for the Content
chapters.summary: Hill explains looking for transfer from cognitive-skill practice like attention and short-term memory to math performance, likening cognitive training to a framework that content must adhere to, laments a dumbing down of education, and blames well-meaning liberal teachers who held Black students to lower standards, recalling his educator mother's observation, citing George Bush's phrase about low expectations, and arguing that challenging curricula, not remediation, are what works.

chapters.part: A Psychologist's Fight for the Mind
chapters.title: Everything Is About the Test
chapters.summary: Hill calls Virginia's ironically named Standards of Learning a barrier to innovation in districts like Petersburg where everything turns on test scores, describes Virginia Tech workshops where teachers hungry for African American and civil rights history struggle to fit it into the SOLs, and concludes the factory model demands radical restructuring.

chapters.part: Still Fighting the Same Battle
chapters.title: Where Is the Outrage
chapters.summary: Hill voices optimism that an entrenched educational bureaucracy will change slowly, predicts MOOCs and the decline of the university, recalls inspiring teachers who passed on excitement rather than rote test prep, and frames the present fight as the same battle in a new form: racial segregation become class segregation in disgraceful city schools, with the Iraq war's trillion dollars found while education is neglected, naming education the movement's legacy and asking where the outrage is over school conditions and mass incarceration, citing The New Jim Crow before closing.

loc_video.caption: Oliver W. Hill, Jr., oral history interview conducted by David P. Cline in Petersburg, Virginia, 2013 August 17
